channel.title: The Woman's Journal Digital Archive
channel.description: Recent content on The Woman's Journal Digital Archive

item.title: Lucy Stone
item.description: Lucy Stone was an American abolitionist and women’s rights activist.

item.title: About
item.description:
About The Woman’s Journal

The Woman’s Journal was a weekly newspaper published in Boston, Massachusetts from January 8, 1870 to 1931. It was founded by Lucy Stone and her husband Henry B. Blackwell as the official organ of the American Woman Suffrage Association (AWSA). The paper covered suffrage campaigns, legislative developments, women in the professions, labor issues, and international women’s rights movements.

Lucy Stone, one of the foremost orators of the abolitionist and suffrage movements, served as editor alongside her husband for much of the paper’s early history. The journal stood apart from other suffrage publications in its moderate, pragmatic approach—working state by state toward women’s enfranchisement rather than pursuing a federal constitutional amendment exclusively.

item.title: Themes in the First Year: The Woman's Journal, 1870
The Woman's Journal - December 3, 1870
item.description: Volume 1, Issue 48 of The Woman’s Journal offers a retrospective on the paper’s first year of publication, surveying the state of the suffrage movement at the close of 1870. The editors reflect on legislative gains and setbacks, celebrate the paper’s growing readership, and look ahead to the campaigns of 1871. Full issue transcription and page images are available at the Internet Archive via the link above.

item.title: The Woman's Journal - September 10, 1870
item.description: Volume 1, Issue 36 of The Woman’s Journal grapples with the aftermath of the Fifteenth Amendment’s ratification, which granted voting rights to Black men but explicitly excluded women. The issue reflects on the fractured relationships within the broader reform movement and reaffirms the paper’s commitment to universal suffrage. Full issue transcription and page images are available at the Internet Archive via the link above.

item.title: The Woman's Journal - June 11, 1870
item.description: Volume 1, Issue 23 of The Woman’s Journal turns attention to women in the workforce, documenting the labor conditions faced by women in industrial trades and domestic service. The issue argues for equal pay and the right of women to organize. Full issue transcription and page images are available at the Internet Archive via the link above.

item.title: The Woman's Journal - March 26, 1870
item.description: Volume 1, Issue 12 of The Woman’s Journal covers ongoing suffrage campaigns and features reporting on the state of women’s education and access to higher learning. Articles in this issue address legislative efforts in several states and the connection between educational reform and women’s full citizenship. Full issue transcription and page images are available at the Internet Archive via the link above.

item.title: The Woman's Journal - January 8, 1870
item.description: This is the inaugural issue of The Woman’s Journal, launched on January 8, 1870 from offices in Boston, Massachusetts. The first issue set out the paper’s founding mission: to advocate for women’s suffrage and the full equality of women in American civic life. Full issue transcription and page images are available at the Internet Archive via the link above.
